summary: Gruber amplifies complaints from Chris Nandor and Buzz Andersen about Mac OS X's persistent bugs, particularly in the Finder, arguing that Apple keeps shipping flashy new features while fundamental problems remain unfixed. He contends that the OS X Finder is not merely a poor design but a poor design poorly implemented, riddled with minor bugs that have persisted across multiple major releases. He illustrates this with a specific example: AppleScript's inability to reliably get the Finder selection in column view, a bug that has existed for nearly the entire lifespan of OS X. He dismisses the perpetual hype cycle where each new OS X release promises Finder improvements that never materialize, noting Apple has had seven years on the project and deserves no more slack.
key_points: Apple keeps prioritizing showy new features like transparent graphics and piles over fixing fundamental, longstanding bugs in the Finder and other core apps
The OS X Finder suffers from both poor design and poor implementation — the bugs compound the design problems
A specific AppleScript bug where getting the Finder selection fails in column view has been broken across nearly every OS X release
The hype cycle around each new OS X release creates false hope that the Finder will finally be fixed, deflecting criticism into speculation about the next version
The classic Mac OS Finder may not have been universally loved, but it was reliable and not buggy in the way the OS X Finder is
money_quotes: The OS X Finder isn't just a poor design — it's a poor design poorly implemented.
Guess what — it's been seven years since Apple started the project. They don't deserve any slack whatsoever regarding the quality of the Finder.
I'd probably shut the fuck up about it if it weren't for all the bugs.

The hype cycle for upcoming major Mac OS X revisions inevitably includes rumors that the Finder will see huge performance improvements. And then the update ships, and the improvements are less than encouraging.
But, hey, why gripe when we can start speculating about the next major Mac OS X update? Surely the Finder will be improved eventually the thinking goes.
Maybe you didn't like the old Finder, but you sure couldn't call it 'buggy'.
Great, so I will be able to have more transparent graphics. Yippee. I just don't care.
key_insight: Apple's pattern of shipping flashy new OS X features while leaving the Finder riddled with longstanding bugs represents a fundamental failure of priorities that no amount of hype-cycle optimism can excuse.
